title: Christ the Redeemer (statue)

infobox.value: [Corcovado mountain](/wiki/Corcovado_mountain "Corcovado mountain"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")

Designed by sculptors [Paul Landowski](/wiki/Paul_Landowski "Paul Landowski") and Heitor da Silva Costa and built by engineer [Heitor da Silva Costa](/wiki/Heitor_da_Silva_Costa "Heitor da Silva Costa") in collaboration with [Albert Caquot](/wiki/Albert_Caquot "Albert Caquot"). Sculptor [Gheorghe Leonida](/wiki/Gheorghe_Leonida "Gheorghe Leonida") created the face.
30 metres (98 ft), 38 metres (125 ft) with its pedestal

***Christ the Redeemer*** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Cristo Redentor*, standard Brazilian Portuguese: [[ˈkɾistu ʁedẽˈtoʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is an [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") statue of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil, created by French-Polish sculptor [Paul Landowski](/wiki/Paul_Landowski "Paul Landowski") and built by Brazilian engineer [Heitor da Silva Costa](/wiki/Heitor_da_Silva_Costa "Heitor da Silva Costa"), in collaboration with French engineer [Albert Caquot](/wiki/Albert_Caquot "Albert Caquot"). [Romanian](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") sculptor [Gheorghe Leonida](/wiki/Gheorghe_Leonida "Gheorghe Leonida") sculpted the face. Constructed between 1922 and 1931, the statue is 30 metres (98 ft) high, excluding its 8-metre (26 ft) [pedestal](/wiki/Pedestal "Pedestal"), and faces east. The arms stretch 28 metres (92 ft) wide.[[1]](#cite_note-brit-1)[[2]](#cite_note-giumbelli2014-2) It is made of [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete "Reinforced concrete") and [soapstone](/wiki/Soapstone "Soapstone").[[3]](#cite_note-Largest_christ-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Travel_Channel-4)[[5]](#cite_note-BBC-5) *Christ the Redeemer* differs considerably from its original design, as the initial plan was a large Christ with a globe in one hand and a cross in the other. Although the project organizers originally accepted the design, it later changed to the statue of today, with the arms spread out wide.

The statue weighs 635 [metric tons](/wiki/Metric_ton "Metric ton") (625 [long](/wiki/Long_ton "Long ton"), 700 [short tons](/wiki/Short_ton "Short ton")), and is located at the peak of the 700-metre (2,300 ft) [Corcovado](/wiki/Corcovado "Corcovado") mountain in the [Tijuca National Park](/wiki/Tijuca_National_Park "Tijuca National Park") overlooking the city of Rio de Janeiro. This statue is the largest [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco")–style [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture") in the world.[[6]](#cite_note-Britannia-6) A symbol of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") around the world, the statue has also become a [cultural icon](/wiki/Cultural_icon "Cultural icon") of both Rio de Janeiro and Brazil and was voted one of the [New 7 Wonders of the World](/wiki/New_7_Wonders_of_the_World "New 7 Wonders of the World").[[7]](#cite_note-Hindustan_times-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_the_Redeemer_(statue)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Marc_Ferrez_-_IMS_007A6P4FP15-015.jpg/250px-Marc_Ferrez_-_IMS_007A6P4FP15-015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marc_Ferrez_-_IMS_007A6P4FP15-015.jpg)

A view of [Corcovado](/wiki/Corcovado "Corcovado") before the construction, between 1880 and 1900

[Vincentian](/wiki/Congregation_of_the_Mission "Congregation of the Mission") priest Pedro Maria Boss first suggested placing a Christian monument on Mount Corcovado in the mid-1850s to honor [Princess Isabel](/wiki/Isabel,_Princess_Imperial_of_Brazil "Isabel, Princess Imperial of Brazil"), regent of Brazil and the daughter of [Emperor Pedro II](/wiki/Emperor_Pedro_II "Emperor Pedro II"), but the project was not approved.[[1]](#cite_note-brit-1) In 1889, the country became a republic, and owing to the [separation of church and state](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state "Separation of church and state"), the proposed statue was dismissed.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Cristo_Redentor_em_constru%C3%A7%C3%A3o_%28013RJ009013%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Cristo_Redentor_em_constru%C3%A7%C3%A3o_%28013RJ009013%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cristo_Redentor_em_constru%C3%A7%C3%A3o_(013RJ009013)_(cropped).jpg)

The peak of Corcovado with the statue under construction, c. 1930−1931

The Catholic Circle of Rio made a second proposal for a landmark statue on the mountain in 1920.[[9]](#cite_note-Riotur-9) The group organized an event called *Semana do Monumento* ("Monument Week") to attract donations and collect signatures to support the building of the statue. The organization was motivated by what they perceived as "Godlessness" in the society. The donations came mostly from [Brazilian Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Brazil "Roman Catholicism in Brazil").[[3]](#cite_note-Largest_christ-3) The designs considered for the "Statue of the Christ" included a representation of the [Christian cross](/wiki/Christian_cross "Christian cross"), a statue of Jesus with a globe in his hands, and a pedestal symbolizing the world.[[10]](#cite_note-JB-10) Eventually, the statue of Christ the [Redeemer](/wiki/Redeemer_(Christianity) "Redeemer (Christianity)") with open arms, a symbol of peace, was chosen.

Local engineer [Heitor da Silva Costa](/wiki/Heitor_da_Silva_Costa "Heitor da Silva Costa") and artist Carlos Oswald designed the statue.[[11]](#cite_note-11) French sculptor [Paul Landowski](/wiki/Paul_Landowski "Paul Landowski") created the work.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 1922, Landowski commissioned fellow Parisian [Romanian](/wiki/Romania "Romania") sculptor [Gheorghe Leonida](/wiki/Gheorghe_Leonida "Gheorghe Leonida"), who studied sculpture at the Fine Arts Conservatory in Bucharest and in Italy.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Visita_de_estudantes_ao_Cristo_Redentor%2C_Acervo_do_Instituto_Moreira_Salles.jpg/250px-Visita_de_estudantes_ao_Cristo_Redentor%2C_Acervo_do_Instituto_Moreira_Salles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Visita_de_estudantes_ao_Cristo_Redentor,_Acervo_do_Instituto_Moreira_Salles.jpg)

*Christ the Redeemer* in 1931

A group of engineers and technicians studied Landowski's submissions, and they felt building the structure out of [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete "Reinforced concrete") (designed by [Albert Caquot](/wiki/Albert_Caquot "Albert Caquot")) instead of steel was more suitable for the [cross](/wiki/Cross "Cross")-shaped statue. The concrete making up the base was supplied from [Limhamn](/wiki/Limhamn "Limhamn"), Sweden.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The outer layers are [soapstone](/wiki/Soapstone "Soapstone"), chosen for its enduring qualities and ease of use.[[4]](#cite_note-Travel_Channel-4) Construction took nine years, from 1922 to 1931, and cost the equivalent of US$250,000 (equivalent to $4,500,000 in 2025) and the monument opened on October 12, 1931.[[4]](#cite_note-Travel_Channel-4)[[5]](#cite_note-BBC-5) During the opening ceremony, the statue was to be lit by a battery of [floodlights](/wiki/Floodlight "Floodlight") turned on remotely by Italian [shortwave radio](/wiki/Shortwave_radio "Shortwave radio") inventor [Guglielmo Marconi](/wiki/Guglielmo_Marconi "Guglielmo Marconi"), stationed 9,200 kilometres (5,700 mi) away in Rome but because of bad weather, the lights were activated on site.[[9]](#cite_note-Riotur-9)

In October 2006, on the 75th anniversary of the statue's completion, [Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)") [Eusebio Oscar Scheid](/wiki/Eusebio_Oscar_Scheid "Eusebio Oscar Scheid"), [Archbishop](/wiki/Archbishop "Archbishop") of Rio, consecrated a [chapel](/wiki/Chapel "Chapel"), named after Brazil's patron saint—[Our Lady of the Apparition](/wiki/Nossa_Senhora_Aparecida "Nossa Senhora Aparecida")—under the statue, allowing Catholics to hold baptisms and weddings there.[[5]](#cite_note-BBC-5)

Lightning struck the statue during a violent thunderstorm on February 10, 2008, causing some damage to the fingers, head and eyebrows. The [Rio de Janeiro state](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") government initiated a restoration effort to replace some of the outer soapstone layers and repair the lightning rods on the statue. Lightning damaged it again on January 17, 2014, dislodging a finger on the right hand.[[16]](#cite_note-Estadão.com.br-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Último_Segundo(2)-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Canteiro_de_Obras-18)[[19]](#cite_note-The_Age_newspaper-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Est%C3%A1tua_do_Cristo_Redentor_nas_cores_da_Bandeira_do_Brasil.jpg/250px-Est%C3%A1tua_do_Cristo_Redentor_nas_cores_da_Bandeira_do_Brasil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Est%C3%A1tua_do_Cristo_Redentor_nas_cores_da_Bandeira_do_Brasil.jpg)

The statue lit in the colors of the [Flag of Brazil](/wiki/Flag_of_Brazil "Flag of Brazil")

In 2010, a massive restoration of the statue began. Work included cleaning, replacing the mortar and soapstone on the exterior, restoring iron in the internal structure, and waterproofing the monument. Vandals attacked the statue during renovation, spraying paint along the arm. Mayor [Eduardo Paes](/wiki/Eduardo_Paes "Eduardo Paes") called the act "a crime against the nation". The culprits later apologized and presented themselves to the police.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Globo_vandalism-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In reference to Brazilian striker [Ronaldo](/wiki/Ronaldo_(Brazilian_footballer) "Ronaldo (Brazilian footballer)")'s usual [goal celebration](/wiki/Goal_celebration "Goal celebration") of both arms outstretched, the [Pirelli](/wiki/Pirelli "Pirelli") tyre company ran a 1998 commercial in which he replaced the statue while in an [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") strip.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The commercial was controversial with the Catholic Church.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Restoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_the_Redeemer_(statue)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Restoration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Christ_on_Corcovado_mountain.JPG/250px-Christ_on_Corcovado_mountain.JPG)](/wiki/File:Christ_on_Corcovado_mountain.JPG)

A panoramic view of the statue at the top of Corcovado Mountain with [Sugarloaf Mountain](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Mountain "Sugarloaf Mountain") (centre) and [Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay "Guanabara Bay") in the background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Rio_Larson_3.jpg/250px-Rio_Larson_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_Larson_3.jpg)

*Christ the Redeemer* aerial view with [Tijuca Forest](/wiki/Tijuca_Forest "Tijuca Forest")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Morro_do_Corcovado.jpg/250px-Morro_do_Corcovado.jpg)](/wiki/File:Morro_do_Corcovado.jpg)

*Christ the Redeemer* atop Corcovado with Sugarloaf Mountain in background

In 1990, several organizations, including the [Archdiocese of Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_S%C3%A3o_Sebasti%C3%A3o_do_Rio_de_Janeiro "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro"), media company [Grupo Globo](/wiki/Grupo_Globo "Grupo Globo"), oil company Shell do Brasil, environmental regulator [IBAMA](/wiki/Brazilian_Institute_of_Environment_and_Renewable_Natural_Resources "Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources"), [National Institute of Historic and Artistic Heritage](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Historic_and_Artistic_Heritage "National Institute of Historic and Artistic Heritage"), and the city government of Rio de Janeiro entered into an agreement to conduct restoration work.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

More work on the statue and its environs was conducted in 2003 and early 2010. In 2003, a set of [escalators](/wiki/Escalator "Escalator"), [walkways](/wiki/Walkway "Walkway"), and [elevators](/wiki/Elevator "Elevator") were installed to facilitate access to the platform surrounding the statue. The four-month restoration in 2010[[26]](#cite_note-BBC,_2010-07-01-26) focused on the statue itself. The statue's internal structure was renovated and its soapstone mosaic covering was restored by removing a crust of fungi and other microorganisms and repairing small cracks. The [lightning rods](/wiki/Lightning_rod "Lightning rod") located in the statue's head and arms were also repaired, and new lighting fixtures were installed at the foot of the statue.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The restoration involved one hundred people and used more than 60,000 pieces of stone taken from the same [quarry](/wiki/Quarry "Quarry") as the original statue.[[26]](#cite_note-BBC,_2010-07-01-26) During the unveiling of the restored statue, it was illuminated with green-and-yellow lighting in support of the [Brazil national football team](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team") playing in the [2010 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup").[[26]](#cite_note-BBC,_2010-07-01-26)

Maintenance work needs to be conducted periodically because of the [strong winds](/wiki/Aeolian_processes "Aeolian processes") and [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion") to which the statue is exposed, as well as lightning strikes.[[28]](#cite_note-Veja_Rio_(27_January_2010)-28) The original pale stone is no longer available in sufficient quantity, and replacement stones are increasingly darker in hue.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
